text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION -- FOLLOWING 4 CYCLES OF Yervoy/Opdivo INDUCTION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Raymond Kowalski, 63-year-old male with known Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (right kidney primary, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation), status post cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. The patient subsequently initiated combination immunotherapy with ipi 1 mg/kg and nivo 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles for residual bilateral pulmonary metastases and retroperitoneal nodal disease. This examination is performed for routine restaging following completion of the induction phase, approximately 3 months after systemic therapy initiation. Reference prior examinations: staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (initial presentation) and post-surgical CT obtained prior to immunotherapy initiation.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of non-ionic iodinated intravenous contrast material (iohexol). Acquisitions were obtained in the portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations were reviewed in conjunction with axial images. Slice thickness: 3 mm (chest), 3 mm (abdomen/pelvis). No enteric contrast administered. Images are compared to the prior staging CT and the pre-therapy MRI abdomen.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINDINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally. There is no endobronchial lesion identified. The lungs are otherwise clear without consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural-based nodularity to suggest immunotherapy-related pneumonitis. Specifically, no diffuse or patchy ground-glass opacities are appreciated in the perihilar or peripheral lung zones. No air trapping or mosaic attenuation pattern.

Pulmonary Nodules (Previously Documented Metastatic Lesions):
-- Right lower lobe: A previously documented nodule in the posterior basal segment of the right lower lobe has decreased in size from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm in greatest axial dimension. The nodule retains its rounded morphology with smooth margins; it now appears less conspicuous on lung windows. This represents a meaningful decrease in size compared to the staging examination.
-- Left lower lobe: A previously noted nodule in the posterior basal segment of the left lower lobe remains grossly stable, measuring 0.8 cm (unchanged from prior). The nodule maintains rounded margins without internal change.
-- Right upper lobe: A previously documented small nodule in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe has decreased from 0.6 cm to approximately 0.4 cm in greatest dimension and is now borderline measurable on this examination. No new nodules are identified in the right upper lobe.
-- No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No cavitating lesions. No satellite nodules surrounding the known lesions.

Pleura:
No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or pleural-based masses.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The great vessels are unremarkable. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy above the threshold for enlargement. The trachea is midline. The esophagus appears normal in caliber.

Chest Wall:
No chest wall masses or rib destruction. No axillary lymphadenopathy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size with homogeneous attenuation. No focal hepatic lesion identified. The portal and hepatic veins are patent. The biliary system is not dilated. No periportal edema.

Gallbladder and Bile Ducts:
The gallbladder is present without calculi, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct is not dilated.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in size and attenuation with no ductal dilation, focal mass, or peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
The left adrenal gland appears normal. The right adrenal gland is absent, consistent with prior right adrenalectomy performed at the time of cytoreductive nephrectomy.

Kidneys:
The right renal fossa is postoperative in appearance, consistent with prior right radical nephrectomy. There is expected postoperative soft tissue stranding and small volume fluid in the right renal fossa without a discrete mass to suggest local recurrence at this time. The left kidney is present with normal size, contour, and parenchymal enhancement. No left-sided hydronephrosis or renal mass identified.

Retroperitoneal and Mesenteric Lymph Nodes:
In the upper retroperitoneum, within the aortocaval region, there is a lobular soft tissue nodal conglomerate measuring approximately 2.1 cm in short-axis dimension in the upper para-aortic/aortocaval region. This represents NEW nodal disease compared to the prior staging examination; this region was surgically sampled and was not described as a discrete residual nodal mass on the post-nephrectomy imaging. The nodule has rounded contours with slightly heterogeneous enhancement. The right para-aortic surgical bed demonstrates postoperative change without a clearly defined residual nodal mass in the exact location previously dissected, though the distinction from postoperative fibrosis is limited.

Gastrointestinal:
The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are unremarkable without wall thickening, obstruction, or inflammatory change. No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.

Aorta and Vessels:
The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and renal arteries are patent. The inferior vena cava is patent without filling defect.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PELVIS:

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
A rounded, well-defined soft tissue nodal mass measuring approximately 1.6 cm in greatest dimension is identified along the right common iliac chain. This nodule demonstrates intermediate attenuation with mild heterogeneous post-contrast enhancement. This lymph node was NOT present on prior staging imaging and represents new nodal disease in the pelvis not previously documented. The left iliac chain and inguinal regions appear unremarkable without pathologically enlarged nodes.

Pelvic Organs:
The bladder is normally distended without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defect. The prostate gland is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic enlargement, without a focal lesion. The seminal vesicles appear symmetric and unremarkable. No free pelvic fluid.

Bone Windows:
The imaged osseous structures, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, iliac bones, and proximal femora, demonstrate no lytic or sclerotic lesions to suggest skeletal metastasis. No pathologic fractures are identified. Multilevel degenerative changes are present in the lumbar spine consistent with the patient's age.

Soft Tissue:
No suspicious soft tissue masses are identified in the imaged regions. No subcutaneous nodules.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. DISCORDANT/MIXED RESPONSE TO IMMUNOTHERAPY:
   The pulmonary metastatic nodules show a favorable response since initiation of Yervoy/Opdivo. The right lower lobe nodule has appreciably decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm, and the right upper lobe nodule has also decreased from 0.6 cm to approximately 0.4 cm. The left lower lobe nodule is stable. No new pulmonary metastases are identified. The pulmonary disease is clearly responding to the current immunotherapy regimen.

2. NEW RETROPERITONEAL NODAL DISEASE:
   There is a new 2.1 cm aortocaval nodal conglomerate in the upper retroperitoneum not present on prior imaging. This represents emergence of nodal disease in this region, which could reflect either true progression of nodal metastatic disease in the surgical bed, or possibly an unusual response pattern. Given the heterogeneous enhancement pattern, metastatic involvement is favored over inflammatory/reactive etiology.

3. NEW PELVIC NODAL DISEASE:
   A new 1.6 cm right common iliac chain lymph node has appeared since the staging examination. This was not present on any prior imaging and represents new disease in a nodal station not previously involved. The morphology and enhancement characteristics are consistent with nodal metastatic involvement in the setting of this patient's known metastatic clear cell RCC.

4. No evidence of hepatic, osseous (within imaged skeleton), or central nervous system (not imaged) metastases. No adrenal metastasis in the retained left adrenal gland. The right renal fossa does not demonstrate convincing local recurrence.

5. No imaging features of immune-related pneumonitis, colitis, or other immune-mediated organ toxicity.

Summary: This examination documents a mixed response pattern -- the bilateral pulmonary nodules are decreasing in size and responding to Yervoy/nivo, while new retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal lesions have emerged. Clinical and oncologic correlation with multidisciplinary discussion is warranted to determine whether this pattern represents pseudoprogression, discordant true response, or nodal disease progression in the setting of pulmonary response.
